tldr: Part I of a six-paper condensed-mathematics program on topological phases of matter. It builds the analytic ground floor for the whole series: G-symmetric interactions of finite F-norm form a real Banach space B_F whose Heisenberg dynamics obeys a Lieb-Robinson bound (Thm I-A, repackaging Nachtergaele-Sims-Young); this dynamics is a genuine morphism of condensed sets out of the condensed vector space cond(B_F) (Thm I-B, via full faithfulness of condensation on compactly generated spaces); and, the paper's load-bearing result, an S-continuous family of interactions over a profinite base is a norm-convergent compatible system over finite quotients whose uniform F-norm bound forces one common Lieb-Robinson velocity across the entire base (Thm I-C). Three explicitly numbered conjectures (I-1 condensed higher stack, I-2 condensed group of quasi-local automorphisms, I-3 descent along a disorder hull) mark what remains open, and an accompanying Haskell/QuickCheck simulation of the transverse-field Ising chain exhibits the light cone and fits its velocity.
plain_language_summary:
A quantum lattice system -- for instance a chain of spins or qubits, or a lattice model of a topological insulator -- consists of a grid of sites, each carrying a small quantum system, coupled by local interaction terms that decay with distance. This paper is the opening installment of a six-part mathematical research program that reworks the classification of 'topological phases' of such systems using 'condensed mathematics' (Clausen and Scholze's framework for handling continuously varying families, profinite limits, and disordered systems more flexibly than ordinary topology). Before any of that machinery can be brought to bear, someone has to nail down the underlying physics rigorously and show it is compatible with the condensed framework -- that is this paper's entire job.

The physical fact at the center of the paper is a 'speed limit' for quantum information on a lattice: under time evolution generated by a local Hamiltonian, a disturbance at one site cannot influence a distant site arbitrarily quickly -- there is a finite propagation velocity and an effective light cone. This is the Lieb-Robinson bound, proved originally in 1972 and given its modern, quantitative packaging (via a decay-rate function called an 'F-function') by Nachtergaele, Sims, and Young. The paper's first proven result is that the interactions obeying a fixed decay rate form a genuine complete normed vector space (a Banach space), and that the time evolution built from them is continuous with respect to that norm -- not new physics, but a careful, fully-proved repackaging of established results.

The reason this continuity matters is that 'condensed mathematics' only recognizes an assignment as a genuine morphism between its objects if the assignment is continuous in an honest topology. The paper's second proven result is exactly this upgrade: because the Lieb-Robinson estimate is a continuity statement in the Banach norm, the passage from an interaction (and a time) to the resulting quantum evolution is not just a rule but a bona fide morphism between condensed sets.

The paper's central and most consequential result concerns families of systems rather than single ones. Suppose the interactions vary continuously over a 'profinite' parameter space -- a compact space built as a limit of finite approximations, which is exactly the right structure for encoding physical disorder or a hierarchy of finite-volume approximations. The paper shows that if the total interaction strength is bounded uniformly across the whole family, then the entire family shares one common propagation velocity and light cone -- not merely a velocity that happens to be finite at each individual point but possibly blows up across the family. This upgrade from 'finite pointwise' to 'uniformly finite across the whole base' is what allows families of physical systems, including disordered ones, to be treated as single well-behaved objects in the condensed-mathematical sense, and the paper draws out two immediate physical payoffs: a uniform version of the standard fact that a spectral gap implies exponentially decaying correlations, and the technical machinery (quasi-adiabatic continuation) used elsewhere in the series to say when two gapped systems count as 'the same phase.'

Throughout, the paper is careful to separate what is actually proved from what is merely proposed. Three results are proved in full (labeled Theorems I-A, I-B, and I-C); three further claims about deeper structure -- that the space of all such systems forms a richer gluing structure called a stack, that the symmetries assemble into a well-behaved group, and that the estimates can be reconstructed purely from finite-resolution approximations -- are stated as explicit, numbered open conjectures rather than asserted as fact. The paper closes with a concrete computational illustration: a Haskell program simulates a simple one-dimensional spin chain (the transverse-field Ising model), plots the predicted light cone, and fits the propagation velocity from the simulation, as a sanity check on the finite-volume estimate that underlies (but cannot by itself verify) the paper's infinite-volume and condensed-mathematical claims.
key_contributions: Proves Theorem I-A: for a fixed F-function, the G-symmetric interactions of finite F-norm form a real Banach space B_F, and the associated Heisenberg dynamics is a strongly continuous one-parameter group of *-automorphisms of the quasi-local algebra obeying a Lieb-Robinson bound, including a complete, non-cited proof that B_F is complete (Prop. 'banach-space') via an explicit operator-norm Cauchy argument.

Proves Theorem I-B: the condensation of B_F is a condensed R-vector space and the assignment (t, Phi) to tau^Phi_t is a morphism of condensed sets from cond(R) x cond(B_F) to cond(Aut(A)), obtained from full faithfulness of condensation on compactly generated (in particular metrizable, Banach) spaces, and refined to a morphism of light condensed sets when interactions are drawn from a separable subspace.

Proves Theorem I-C, the paper's central analytic result: over a profinite base S, an S-continuous family of interactions is exactly a norm-convergent compatible system of locally constant families over finite quotients of S, and a uniform F-norm bound forces a single Lieb-Robinson velocity and light cone valid simultaneously for every point of the base, rather than only a pointwise-finite velocity at each point separately.

Derives, as complete-proof consequences of the uniform bound, uniform exponential clustering of ground-state correlations under a uniform spectral gap (Prop. 'clustering', explicitly flagged as conditional given the Cubitt-Perez-Garcia-Wolf undecidability of deciding gappedness) and the quasi-adiabatic-continuation / automorphic-equivalence generators of the equivalence class W that later parts of the series invert to define phases (Prop. 'qac', via Hastings-Wen and Bachmann-Michalakis-Nachtergaele-Sims).

States three explicitly numbered, clearly-flagged-as-open conjectures marking the edge of current technique (I-1: the moduli problem Ham_{d,G} is a condensed higher stack, not merely a condensed set; I-2: quasi-local automorphisms assemble into a condensed group with quasi-adiabatic continuation as internal path-lifting; I-3: Lieb-Robinson estimates satisfy descent along finite quotients of a profinite disorder hull), and supplies an accompanying Haskell/QuickCheck package that simulates the transverse-field Ising chain, visualizes the light cone, fits its velocity, and checks the paper's elementary inequalities (F-function monotonicity, reweighting bounds, subadditivity of the F-norm).
audience: Mathematical physicists and operator algebraists working on rigorous quantum lattice dynamics, Lieb-Robinson bounds, and quasi-locality; researchers in condensed, analytic, or pyknotic mathematics (the Clausen-Scholze program) interested in applications of the formalism outside arithmetic geometry; and readers of the companion six-part series (Parts II-VI) who need this paper's Banach-space and uniform-light-cone results as the locality-layer input to positivity, gap stability, effective-field-theory equivalence, and realizability. The introduction, main theorem statements, and transverse-field Ising worked example are largely self-contained for anyone with graduate-level background in either operator-algebraic quantum lattice systems or condensed mathematics, though full appreciation of the condensed-morphism framing benefits from some prior exposure to Clausen-Scholze.
